perturbation.original_text: Both LEO and GALEN shall observe all applicable Laws in effect in fulfilling their obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: LEO and GALEN shall use reasonable efforts to observe applicable Laws in fulfilling their obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall observe' to 'shall use reasonable efforts to observe', the contract weakens the commitment to comply with all applicable laws, potentially creating a loophole. This is especially problematic for laws relating to pharmaceutical development and marketing, where strict adherence is often required by regulatory bodies like the FDA.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (21 U.S.C. § 331) prohibits the introduction or delivery for introduction into interstate commerce of any drug that is adulterated or misbranded. By weakening the obligation to observe all applicable laws, the modified text creates a potential loophole for non-compliance with the FD&C Act, increasing the legal risk.

perturbation.justification: The original contract requires strict adherence to applicable laws ('shall observe'). The changed text weakens this obligation by only requiring 'reasonable efforts' to observe these laws. The Law Explanation states this creates a loophole for non-compliance, specifically mentioning the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (21 U.S.C. § 331). Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates a 'Page Not Found' error, the Perturbation Explanation supports the idea that weakening compliance with laws related to pharmaceutical development and marketing could be problematic. Since the obligation to comply with all laws is replaced with a 'reasonable efforts' standard, this creates a legal contradiction as it undermines mandatory compliance. The change falls under the Omission Legal Contradiction definition because a legally mandated level of consumer protection through pharmaceutical regulation has been effectively removed by the change.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party hereto hereby waives to the fullest extent permitted by applicable Law, any right it may have to a trial by jury in respect to any litigation directly or indirectly arising out of, under or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party hereto waives any right it may have to a trial by jury in respect to any litigation directly or indirectly arising out of, under or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'to the fullest extent permitted by applicable Law' implies that the waiver of jury trial is absolute and unconditional. However, some state laws may place limits on the enforceability of jury trial waivers, especially in certain types of disputes. This change could render the waiver unenforceable in situations where the law does not permit a full waiver.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 4102(c)
perturbation.law_explanation: New York CPLR § 4102(c) outlines specific procedures and limitations regarding the waiver of a jury trial. Removing the language 'to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law' suggests an unconditional waiver, which might not be enforceable under NY law if the specific requirements of CPLR § 4102(c) are not strictly met (e.g., conspicuousness, knowing and voluntary waiver).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules Law § 4102 (2022) - Demand and Waiver of Trial by Jury; Specification of Issues. :: 2022 New York Laws :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the New York Laws 2024 N.Y. Laws 2023 N.Y. Laws 2022 N.Y. Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws CVP - Civil Practice Law and Rules Article 41 - Trial by a Jury 4102 - Demand and Waiver of Trial by Jury; Specification of Issues. Universal Citation: NY CPLR § 4102 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 4102. Demand and waiver of trial by jury; specification of issues. (a) Demand. Any party may demand a trial by jury of any issue of fact triable of right by a jury, by serving upon all other parties and filing a note of issue containing a demand for trial by jury. Any party served with a note of issue not containing such a demand may demand a trial by jury by serving upon each party a demand for a trial by jury and filing such demand in the office where the note of issue was filed within fifteen days after service of the note of issue. A demand shall not be accepted for filing unless a note of issue is filed in the action. If no party shall demand a trial by jury as provided herein, the right to trial by jury shall be deemed waived by all parties. A party may not withdraw a demand for trial by jury without the consent of the other parties, regardless of whether another party previously filed a note of issue without a demand for trial by jury. (b) Specification of issues. In his demand a party may specify the issues which he wishes tried by jury; otherwise he shall be deemed to have demanded trial by jury of all issues so triable. If he has demanded trial by jury of only some of the issues, any other party within ten days after service of the demand may serve and file a demand for trial by jury of any other issues in th
perturbation.justification: The change removes the phrase 'to the fullest extent permitted by applicable Law', suggesting an unconditional waiver of jury trial rights. The Law Explanation highlights that New York CPLR § 4102(c) has specific requirements for waiving a jury trial. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing NY CPLR § 4102, discusses demand and waiver of jury trials. The original text acknowledged potential legal limits on the waiver, while the modified text does not, creating a contradiction by potentially waiving rights beyond what is legally permissible.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that Force Majeure has occurred and is continuing for a period of at least six (6) months, the Other Party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement upon thirty (30) days' notice.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that Force Majeure has occurred, the Other Party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the condition that Force Majeure must continue for at least six months before termination, the contract allows for immediate termination upon any Force Majeure event, regardless of its duration. This could contradict laws related to contract stability and good faith, particularly in jurisdictions that require a reasonable opportunity to cure a breach before termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York UCC § 2-615 addresses excuse by failure of presupposed conditions (force majeure). While it allows for excused performance, it implies a standard of reasonableness and good faith in dealing with such events. By allowing immediate termination without a reasonable period to address the force majeure, the altered clause could be seen as violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under New York law, particularly if the force majeure event is minor or easily resolved.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 38 Uniform Commercial Code ARTICLE 2 Sales PART 6 Breach, Repudiation and Excuse previous SECTION 2-614 Substituted Performance up PART 6 Breach, Repudiation and Excuse next SECTION 2-616 Procedure on Notice Claiming Excuse This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-615 Excuse by Failure of Presupposed Conditions Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 6 Section 2--615. Excuse by Failure of Presupposed Conditions. Except so far as a seller may have assumed a greater obligation and subject to the preceding section on substituted performance: (a) Delay in delivery or non-delivery in whole or in part by a seller who complies with paragraphs (b) and (c) is not a breach of his duty under a contract for sale if performance as agreed has been made impracticable by the occurrence of a contingency the non-occurrence of which was a basic assumption on which the contract was made or by compliance in good faith with any applicable foreign or domestic governmental regulation or order whether or not it later proves to be invalid. (b) Where the causes mentioned in paragraph (a) affect only a part of the seller's capacity to perform, he must allocate production and deliveries among his customers but may at his option include regular customers not then under contract as well as his own requirements for further manufacture. He may so allocate in any manner which is fair and reasonable. (c) The seller must notify the buyer seasonably that there will be delay or non-delivery and, when allocation is required under paragraph (b), of the estimated quota thus made available for the buyer.
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the requirement for Force Majeure to continue for at least six months before termination, allowing immediate termination upon any Force Majeure event. This contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under New York law, as explained by the Law Explanation, particularly if the force majeure event is minor or easily resolved. The original contract provided a reasonable timeframe before termination could occur due to force majeure; by removing this requirement, the altered clause potentially violates the standard of reasonableness implied by NY UCC § 2-615, which addresses excuse by failure of presupposed conditions (force majeure). The modified contract fails to allow a reasonable period to address the force majeure, potentially conflicting with the good faith requirement of contract law, even though the snippet does not explicitly discuss contract termination due to Force Majeure. The omission creates a legal loophole.
